data.title: Eileen Gu’s golden secret: ‘train like you’ve never won, compete like you’ve never lost’
data.description:
Eileen Gu kept her perfect record at the Genting Resort Secret Garden intact on Saturday, battling challenging conditions and some brief self-doubt to claim gold in the first Halfpipe World Cup of the 2025-26 season.
The double Olympic gold medallist has never lost on the course that hosted the Beijing Games almost four years ago, and Gu laid down the kind of performance that had already earned her 18 World Cup titles heading into the weekend.
Still, it needed an exceptional final run amid challenging weather that affected the speed and amplitude of multiple skiers.
Before the 22-year-old Chinese Olympian’s last-ditch attempt, it was Great Britain’s Zoe Atkin who looked to be in the driver’s seat after she scored a 90.25 in her first run of the final, five points better than her rival’s opening score.
But with her back against the wall, Gu did not hold back and produced a string of tricks to post a winning score of 91.75.

“That was not easy. I’ve been struggling with speed all of training,” Gu said. “I actually almost didn’t compete because I was worried I wouldn’t be able to do my tricks at the amplitude I was at. That was the first full run that I’ve landed all day. It was such a mental battle.”
Australia’s Indra Brown finished third behind Atkin on 82.00 to claim her first World Cup podium in what was the 15-year-old’s World Cup debut.
Gu’s win comes just a week after she claimed gold in the inaugural Snow League Freeski competition at the same venue.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by FIS Park & Pipe World Cup (@fisparkandpipe)



“I’m less of an ‘affirmations’ person and more of an ‘evidence’ person,” Gu said. “What I mean by that is, find the right thing to do and do so much volume that it’s unreasonable to fail.
“So I’ve been training so much, I’ve been working so hard and every single time I stay the extra hour, do the extra run, it’s proof to myself, it’s evidence to myself that I’m a winner and I deserve to win. So just building back confidence, slowly.”
With a record 19 World Cup wins and three Olympic medals to her name – gold in halfpipe and big air, and silver in slopestyle – Gu said training had always been the secret to her success.
“I train like I’ve never won, and I compete like I’ve never lost,” she said.

data.title: Hong Kong government ‘confident’ of finding new site to rehouse fire victims
data.description:
Hong Kong authorities have expressed confidence in swiftly identifying a location in Tai Po to build subsidised flats as part of resettlement options being reviewed for victims left homeless by the deadly inferno last month.
Deputy Financial Secretary Michael Wong Wai-lun on Saturday outlined several options to resettle displaced residents in Wang Fuk Court, while underscoring the importance of gauging their views first at this stage.
Some options considered were purchasing property rights from homeowners, prioritising displaced residents for subsidised flats under the Home Ownership Scheme (HOS), building new flats in Tai Po, and allowing those eligible to live in public housing.
Wong, who heads the new government task force on providing emergency accommodation arrangements for those affected by the blaze, said the government originally had no plan to build any subsidised flats in Tai Po in the next few years but this could change following the tragedy.
“We have looked into this and we are confident that we can find a place in Tai Po to build these units as soon as possible,” he said.
Wong said it usually took around five years to complete a HOS project from scratch. He said he was confident that the government could speed things up for Wang Fuk Court victims, but stopped short of offering a time frame.
Affected residents could consider other subsidised flats in other districts, which would save them from having to wait several years for resettlement, he added.
Wong also said the government was “seriously” looking into the option of repurchasing the property rights of the flats affected by the blaze, but how to value the properties was a challenge.
“Before the fire, Wang Fuk Court had some transactions with prices of flats listed. But after the fire, everyone can understand that they cannot do any normal transactions. So it is not practical to talk about the market value today,” he said.

Asked if the government would use the money from its support fund set up for the fire victims – which contribution reached around HK$3.6 billion (US$462.5 million) – to compensate homeowners, Wong said authorities would need to further examine the idea.
The official pointed out that nearly HK$3 billion from the fund had yet to be planned to be used for anything, but dividing this over some 1,000 households would mean that they would only get around HK$1 million each.
“I know that a lot of homeowners would want the price that we purchase their property rights to be even higher; we understand this desire, but this would mean that public funds would have to be involved,” he said.
Wong said the government would have to consider the opinions of society and the views of residents before coming to a decision on that.
Authorities would now prioritise collecting views from the displaced residents, he said, adding that it might take time for some households to reach a decision.
Asked whether the government would consider resettling residents back at Wang Fuk Court after the charred buildings had been repaired, Wong said that such an option would also take “a long period of time” as the seven buildings affected in the blaze could be demolished.
“These buildings may not have an immediate risk of collapsing today, but it does not mean they can be reasonably repaired; we really have to look at this again,” he said. “Government experts are working to have a more certain answer in the next few weeks.”
As to whether the buildings could be redeveloped, Wong said the option might take an “extremely long amount of time” and it would depend on many factors such as some residents not opting to return due to the psychological trauma of the fire.
“At this moment, I would not dismiss the possibility of any resettlement solution, but this would definitely not be one that is very quick or easy,” he said.
Separately, Wong and Deputy Chief Secretary Warner Cheuk Wing-hing also criticised people who attempted to profit from the Wang Fuk Court fire following reports of attempts to buy flats at the housing estate for low prices.
Wong urged affected residents to be vigilant and that such transactions were “very suspicious”, adding that the government was monitoring the situation and law enforcement could investigate the matter if needed.

data.title: From climate change to health, global problems need investors to step up
data.description:
People love to hate governments, and they loathe having to pay taxes. However, from now on, we’ll have to embrace more state intervention and the need for higher taxes or else legislate a more dirigiste system of directing personal savings into investment that isn’t so dependent on markets.
This can be either done preemptively or in response to the next financial crisis. It goes without saying that the former course of action is surely preferable to the trauma that will be experienced in the aftermath of the latter.
Capitalist economies don’t necessarily have to embrace socialism, but they must adopt a more rational, structured approach to meeting the overall financial needs of their societies.
Why are such evolutionary changes – or revolutionary in the event of another crisis – essential? The socioeconomic and security needs of market economies are outpacing the ability of existing systems to provide for them.
US President Donald Trump is pressuring American allies around the world to step up military spending, or “defence” spending as it is often euphemistically termed, to levels that will strain their ability to fund other public services. Some are complying, albeit under stress.
Meanwhile, the World Bank is urging member governments to up their health spending to meet the organisation’s goal to “help deliver affordable, quality health services to 1.5 billion people by 2030”. According to the bank’s 2025 Global Monitoring Report, essential health services are out of reach for 4.6 billion people, and 2.1 billion people face financial hardship because of medical expenses.
“These challenges underscore the need for long-term coordinated reforms that help countries build more resilient and equitable health systems,” the World Bank notes.

Demands on governments related to health and defence say nothing of the quickly increasing pressure on funds to finance the growing burden of state pensions for ageing populations or of the huge financial needs related to climate change and related disasters, estimated to be around US$200 trillion by 2050.
Meanwhile, the Institute of International Finance has just issued an alert indicating that increasing levels of borrowing by governments, which is globally in the trillions, may not be sustainable as markets become skittish about public debt levels. More than US$26 trillion was added to global debt stockpiles in the first three quarters of 2025, marking a fresh high of nearly US$346 trillion as debt in both mature and emerging markets hit records, according to a report by the Washington-based institute.
“The rise in global debt continues to be concentrated in the government sector,” says the report, adding that China and the United States have recorded the largest increases before France, Italy and Brazil.
The report notes that sovereign borrowers are likely to keep adding to their debt burdens and interest expenses as budget deficits stay elevated and the impact of fiscal stimulus kicks in next year in Japan, China, Germany and the US.


So what are increasingly debt-burdened governments to do? Increase borrowing levels? Not if debt markets can help it. Raise taxes? Unlikely to be acceptable with the public at a time when global economic growth is slowing, prices are rising and artificial intelligence (AI) threatens job security. Central banks could be persuaded, under pressure, to print more money, but that would open the door to hyperinflation.
The fact of the matter is that there is little governments can realistically do beyond looking to financial markets to share the overall burden of economic and social financing more equally. The potential to accomplish this globally – in a geographic and systemic sense – exists, but it will require bold official reforms to achieve a shift in the relative contributions of public and private sectors.
The current situation is quite extraordinary. People pay taxes and expect their governments to be accountable as to how their money is spent, with the sanction being available, at least in some societies, to change governments if the public is not satisfied.
However, there are no such popular controls available when it comes to influencing how, for example, pension fund and insurance contributions are used by asset or fund managers in the private sector.

A vast wealth management industry has developed in the market economies of the United States, Europe, Asia and beyond. In 2022, the value of global financial assets under management was US$98 trillion, according to the Boston Consulting Group, just shy of global gross domestic product.
The fund management industry channels savings – whether invested by individuals or through institutions such as pension funds, mutual funds and life assurance companies – into a wide range of portfolio financial securities via stock and bond markets.
The bulk of this money goes to investment in the stocks and shares of more than 53,000 companies listed on 78 stock exchanges around the world, but the lion’s share is fed into the hungry maw of Wall Street and a few other major stock exchanges. A relative handful of mega enterprises – tech and AI stocks, for example – are the preferred destination for this largesse.
The shift required to transfer part of the burden of financing at the macroeconomic level from taxpayers to investors (often one and the same people) in market economies is admittedly not going to be easy, and it will require a change in the basic structure of finance. But it is essential if we don’t want to bankrupt ourselves.

data.title: Syria’s year of ‘epic’ transformation earns it Trump’s protection
data.description:
A year after the curtain fell on the Assad regime’s five-decade reign of terror, Syria is emerging as a rare beacon of renewal in a conflict-weary region.
President Ahmed al-Sharaa, a former jihadi filled with revolutionary zeal to rebuild his home after 14 years of civil war, has successfully consolidated power and won broad international recognition.
Over the past 12 months, Syria has “transformed from a global pariah to a fully fledged, integral component of the international community”, according to Charles Lister, director of the Syria programme at the Washington-based Middle East Institute.
Speaking at the Doha Forum in Qatar on Monday, Lister said that the world had “rushed to engage and support the new Syria” since Sharaa’s transitional government was formed in March, buoyed by the promise that a “stable, responsible and recovering” nation could anchor the Middle East and help bring peace and prosperity.
In our estimation, it’s heroic and epic what they’ve achieved
Thomas Barrack, US special envoy for Syria
Western governments, in particular, appear encouraged by the transformation. “In our estimation, it’s heroic and epic what they’ve achieved,” US special envoy for Syria Thomas Barrack told delegates in Doha.
Sitting beside him, Norwegian Foreign Minister Espen Barth Eide agreed. “Let’s not be naive,” he said, noting “the long list of challenges” that the new government still faced. “[But] on balance, this year has been well spent.”
Eide added that the international community had “got it right” by lifting sanctions Sharaa inherited from the Assad era. The last and most restrictive of those measures, imposed by the US under the Caesar Act in 2020, are scheduled to be removed by the end of the month following a rare bipartisan agreement last week in Washington.
That step is expected to clear the way for more than US$28 billion in investment pledges from Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates to fund the reconstruction of Syrian infrastructure shattered by years of war.
Still, the government faces security challenges in the form of Israeli incursions, sectarian armed groups and the remnants of extremist militant networks never fully dismantled.
It must also meet the towering expectations of the hundreds of thousands across Syria who marked the first anniversary of “liberation” last week, celebrating through the night until sleep-deprived security forces shooed them home.
“We still have a long way to go,” Foreign Minister Asaad al‑Shaibani acknowledged at the Doha Forum. But he said the government was “happy” that it had “managed to restore the confidence of the Syrian people”.

Reconstruction will depend on keeping the country free from the conflicts surrounding it and preventing internal strife among its minority communities that resist integration into the new centralised state, often with the help of the government’s foreign adversaries.
“The economy will be our major point of focus in 2026 and the coming years as well,” Shaibani said. “What we fear, however, is the security threats we see.”
Among these he listed the resurgence of Islamic State, the Druze separatists in southern Syria being backed by Israeli incursions and “cross-border militias who are not necessarily satisfied with the changes that have occurred”.
These militias include Iran’s Revolutionary Guards and allied “Axis of Resistance” factions from Iraq and Hezbollah in Lebanon, expelled from Syria after the fall of their ally Bashar al-Assad.
Despite the seizure this year of more than 4,000 weapons systems linked to Iranian-affiliated activity, these groups continue to smuggle arms. Damascus worries that Tehran aims to foment armed unrest among disenfranchised Alawites – the sect that once dominated under Assad.
The Sharaa administration is also anxious to avoid a repeat of the massacre of more than 1,400 Alawites committed by Syrian security forces in March – revenge killings carried out following attacks by Assad loyalists. The first public trials of commanders and other suspects accused of the killings began last month.


Equally pressing is the need to avert another eruption of sectarian violence in the south, where Israeli-armed Druze separatists fought government troops in July, leaving more than 1,000 dead. During those clashes, Israel carried out air strikes on Syria’s defence ministry in Damascus.
Shaibani’s roster of security concerns also includes the Kurdish-led Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF), a US-created coalition of more than 50,000 fighters formed during the civil war to battle both Isis and the Assad regime. The group controls northeastern Syria.
Although Damascus and the SDF agreed in principle in March to fold the militia into the new national army, its commander, Mazloum Abdi, continues to press for full autonomy.
This stance raises the risk of conflict, with both the Syrian state and its close ally Turkey, which views the SDF as the Syrian arm of the Kurdistan Workers’ Party that ended its four-decade insurgency earlier this year and pulled its fighters back to Iraq’s autonomous Kurdish region.

“Whatever happens in Syria and Iraq immediately finds its effect in Turkey,” Turkish Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan told the Doha Forum on Sunday. He said Ankara had demanded the expulsion from Syria of “non-Syrian SDF elements” linked to Turkey, Iraq and Iran, as well as the dismantling of SDF units stationed along the Syrian border.
US diplomatic involvement has so far prevented a direct escalation, but Washington’s patience with the SDF is wearing thin. The same could be said of its tolerance towards Israel’s apparent efforts to undermine the Sharaa administration.
Following the killing of 13 Syrian civilians by Israeli forces in late November, Israel was subjected to a rare public dressing-down from US President Donald Trump, who urged it not to “interfere with Syria’s evolution into a prosperous state”.
His rebuke, delivered via social media on December 1, stood in stark contrast to Washington’s unwavering support for Israeli strikes in southern Lebanon, despite the ceasefire with Hezbollah the US helped broker a year ago.
Eide, Norway’s foreign minister, said Israel should “recognise that we have a Syria that wants to be Syria-led and Syria-owned, with a focus on rebuilding”. He told the Doha Forum that Syria’s new inward focus had severed the direct land bridge between Iran and Hezbollah, a shift he said had strengthened regional security.

Despite this, Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu’s government remains intent on carving out a demilitarised buffer zone across southern Syria.
Ahead of Netanyahu’s coming visit to Washington later this month, which was arranged after Trump’s rebuke, Israeli ministers have been downplaying the prospects of a breakthrough security deal with Syria.
Israeli Diaspora Affairs Minister Amichai Chikli even warned on Tuesday that “war is inevitable”, as Israeli jets resumed overflights and ground incursions following a brief three-day halt during Syria’s “Liberation Day” festivities.
Speaking in Doha a day earlier, Shaibani said his government sought “good relations with everyone, including Israel”, but insisted that no security pact could be reached until Israel withdrew to the 1974 ceasefire line and ceased meddling in Syria’s internal affairs.
“Everything is under process,” US special envoy Barrack told delegates in Qatar, acknowledging that Israel’s recent actions had created “a confusing situation”. He said talks were continuing in cautious “baby steps”.
“It’s a process, not an event,” Barrack added.

data.title: ‘I’ve grown up’: Hong Kong legislator Christine Fong disavows fiery mode of politics
data.description:
Christine Fong Kwok-shan, a long-time district councillor in Hong Kong known for defending constituents’ rights with fiery and sometimes controversial political acts, has pledged to abandon her past protest methods, now that she has been elected as a lawmaker.
The city’s new “Queen of Votes” in the Legislative Council election, who previously positioned herself as an independent moderate belonging neither to the pan-democratic nor pro-establishment bloc, reaffirmed her stance as a “patriot” who was part of the pro-establishment camp, and pledged to “share honour and disgrace” with the government during her tenure.
Fong, 59, finally secured a place in the legislature with her sixth bid over 17 years by winning more than 58,000 votes – the highest number among candidates – in Sunday’s election. The number represented a 42 per cent vote share.
An engineer by trade, she entered politics in the 2000s and has served as a district councillor since 2008.
She made her name as a politician with fiery actions opposing the expansion of a landfill in Tseung Kwan O, the bedrock of her career. She jumped into Victoria Harbour, doused herself in prop blood, staged hunger strikes and was convicted under the Legislative Council (Powers and Privileges) Ordinance twice after protesting in two meetings in the legislature.
“I have grown up. I have been through a lot, and perhaps back then I did not fully consider how the government actually works,” Fong told the Post in an interview on Wednesday.
She said she now believed in advocacy in a “reasoned and measured” way and would no longer resort to radical actions.
“Some of the protest acts I engaged in before are seemingly unnecessary now, because there is a chance to [speak out] through the important platform in Legco.”


Fong said she appreciated that she could now directly engage with government officials, saying she would be pragmatic and realistic instead of “just ranting”.
“Of course, I am an outspoken person. But on livelihood issues, criticism or just slamming [the government] is not a way to solve problems. We should come together and look for solutions.”
Fong had previously promoted herself as a moderate candidate who prioritised livelihood issues, rejecting the binary labels of the pro-establishment and opposition blocs. She was a member of the Liberal Party until 2010 when she left to form the think tank Professional Power the following year.
But she now considered herself part of the pro-establishment camp and the “patriots-only” system, and would “share honour and disgrace” with the government.
Fong has backed down from some of her previous political stances, including a call to vindicate student protesters caught up in the bloody Tiananmen crackdown in 1989.
Asked if residents should be allowed to publicly commemorate those killed in the crackdown, Fong said she “respected” Hongkongers’ freedom but said that the public should “move on”.

Her position on elections has also shifted. Having previously advocated for universal suffrage, Fong lauded Beijing’s “patriots-only” electoral overhaul and refrained from backing outgoing lawmaker Michael Tien Puk-sun’s recent proposal to increase the directly elected seats in the 90-strong legislature from the current 20 to 30.
Stressing political reform was not her top priority, she added: “Of course, I am very much hoping to see more [directly elected seats]. I am definitely someone who is committed to direct election.”
Fong had been vying for a seat in the legislature since 2008, taking part in three general elections and two by-elections.
She came closest to victory in 2016 when she narrowly lost to “Long Hair” Leung Kwok-hung of the now-defunct League of Social Democrats by a razor-thin margin of 1,000 votes.
“Losing like that was extremely painful. Back then, it felt like the harder you worked, the fewer votes you got,” she said.
“But when you fail, you have to stay and keep fighting right where you fell.”
Fong secured more than 58,000 votes in the latest election and attained what she called “nirvana and rebirth” as “Ne Zha”, the fire prince in Chinese folklore whom she played as a young actress in a 1986 television series.

“For over 17 years, I have imagined this moment for a long time, and my dream has finally come true. Achieving what I was determined to do was anything but easy,” she said.
“I feel guilty towards my parents, who passed away three years ago, so the first thing I want to do is to tell them: ‘Your daughter has finally made it.’”
Fong recalled that her parents moved from their home in Kennedy Town to Sai Kung to support her work and put up every poster from her past campaigns at their home.
“They did not often say it out loud, but I can feel [their support],” she said.
“Among all my siblings, my parents worried about me the most, because I poured all my time and resources into serving the community.”
Fong also expressed gratitude to her godparents for their unstinting support, including nonagenarian actor Bowie Wu Fung, whom she said had stayed up until 4am on Monday watching the news to be sure her victory was confirmed.
Some 31.9 per cent of voters cast their ballots in the second poll under the revamped electoral system, slightly above the record low turnout of 30.2 per cent in 2021.

Fong’s stronghold, Sai Kung district, saw the most significant jump in turnout. It had the lowest turnout of 27 per cent among all districts in the last election, but rose to 32 per cent – the highest – this time around.
Fong attributed the increase in voter turnout to residents’ support for professionals such as her, who were willing to roll up their sleeves and work hard for constituents.
Fong said her primary focus when she took office in January would be to address the aftermath of the Tai Po fire, which claimed at least 160 lives.
She aimed to push the government to plug loopholes, standardise safety standards, expand fire safety facilities, and curb dangerous large-scale rigging activities, she said.
She also pledged to ramp up her public engagements to promote Legco’s efforts.

data.title: China eatery owner wears mecha suit to deliver food, dubbed ‘Iron Man’ courier by netizens
data.description:
A Chinese restaurant owner has gone viral for hitting the streets in a home-made mecha suit to deliver food, earning the nickname “Chinese Iron Man” and leaving netizens in awe.
The blogger known as “Gaosai Armour”, who has almost 100,000 followers on social media, recently went viral after posting a video in which he calmly walks into a milk tea shop clad in a metallic suit of armour and picks up his order, leaving stunned onlookers in awe.
A mecha suit is a large mechanical exoskeleton that enhances human strength and capabilities, often resembling a robot. It is normally used in science fiction.
The man behind the armour, Zhou Gaosai, was born in the 1980s and is from Zhengzhou, Henan province in northern China.

He told Cover News that he is not a full-time food delivery worker and just wanted to experience what it felt like to be one.
“Sometimes I even interact with traffic police,” he said. “I ask them whether, wearing this mech suit, I should be using the vehicle lane or the pavement.”
He also shared that the futuristic armour featured in the video was crafted entirely without professional tools.
“I did not have any blueprints or moulds. Every single part was scavenged from a scrapyard,” said Zhou, adding that while each suit costs only a few thousand yuan, it takes about a week of meticulous work to complete, from sourcing components to final assembly.
“The most difficult part is balancing appearance and functionality,” he said.

“I designed it from the feet up, aiming for a hi-tech, intricate look while still ensuring the upper body has enough flexibility. I have revised the design countless times; every detail demands careful thought.
“The moment people surround me to take photographs and call me ‘Iron Man’, it makes everything worth it,” he said.
He revealed that he had been fascinated by science fiction characters since childhood.
In his early years, he wore self-made armour for commercial performances, but due to the modest earnings, he was eventually forced to set aside his dream.
“To make a living, I switched to working in the food and beverage industry, but I could never truly let go of my passion for building mechas,” Zhou said.

“During that time, I asked myself almost every day, can I still return to what I love. From a restaurant owner to a mecha creator, it feels like a restart of my life at 37.”
Zhou said that he hopes to fly in his suit one day and aims to keep innovating, bringing mecha culture into everyday settings.
“I want to tell everyone that it is never too late to chase your dreams. As long as there is passion in your heart, life is full of endless possibilities,” Zhou said.
Chinese netizens have been amazed by his real-life mecha suit.
One person said: “Wait, is this actually wearable? Boss, I want one too.”
“Looks like Iron Man came to China to pick up takeaway,” said another.

data.title: China-built headquarters for 15-member economic bloc strengthens ties with West Africa
data.description:
China is cementing its image as a lasting partner across Africa by gifting high-profile infrastructure, such as presidential palaces and parliament buildings.
A key example is the new US$32 million Economic Community of West African States (Ecowas) headquarters in Abuja. Funded by China and set for handover by the end of January, this centralised complex for the 15-member bloc aims to boost staff productivity and cut operational costs.
On December 4, Chinese ambassador to Nigeria Yu Dunhai visited the site to review progress and met Ecowas Commission President Dr Omar Touray.
Yu hailed the building as a “landmark project of bilateral cooperation” and a strong example of South-South cooperation. Touray, in turn, said the construction, which began in 2022, symbolised “Beijing’s commitment” to regional integration.
The Chinese-built headquarters is being completed amid military takeovers across West Africa’s “coup belt”, including just recently in Guinea-Bissau and an attempted coup in Benin.
Observers said the Ecowas headquarters was a striking example of Chinese “palace or building diplomacy” . Reports show that since 2000, Beijing has bankrolled the construction or renovation of nearly 200 government complexes.
According to a 2020 study by Washington-based think tank the Heritage Foundation, Chinese companies have built or renovated at least 186 government buildings including palaces, parliaments, presidential offices, foreign ministries and military facilities.
That number has since gone up following new projects such as the parliaments in Zimbabwe and the Republic of the Congo, the foreign ministry annex in Ghana and the Africa Centres for Disease Control and Prevention (Africa CDC) headquarters.
The projects were funded through various streams including winning commercial contracts, loans and donations.
However, China’s foreign ministry criticised the report as “filled with falsehoods and ideological bias”.


“China has consistently engaged in practical and efficient cooperation with African nations based on their needs. China has constructed numerous infrastructure projects for African countries, delivering tangible benefits to the African people and creating favourable conditions for international partners to cooperate with Africa,” foreign ministry spokesman Zhao Lijian said after the report was released.
China has built at least one in every three African national parliaments. Observers have suggested that through this strategy, Beijing has been quietly securing influence at the highest levels of governance across the continent.
David Shinn, a China-Africa expert and professor at George Washington University’s Elliott School of International Affairs, said China’s gifting of the Ecowas headquarters in Abuja or the African Union headquarters in Addis Ababa was a clear case of “buying influence with African governments”.
He said the only difference between these gifts and providing a football stadium or parliament building was the fact that China had built influence with many governments and not just one capital.
“While China is not the only country to engage in influence buying by gifting highly visible projects, Beijing does more of it than any other donor government,” Shinn said.
He said these projects were also inherently different from those based on loans or even grants that focused on capacity building in health and education or improving food security.
“Once the structures have become functional, it is up to individual African governments to put their national interests first, which may be hard to do if Beijing presses them to pursue a pro-China position,” Shinn said.
But China’s diplomatic gift extends to other major continental institutions in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia, where China built and funded the African Union (AU) headquarters at a cost of US$200 million, handing it over in 2012.
China built and funded the Africa CDC headquarters, also in Addis Ababa, as a gift for the African Union, valued at about US$80 million.

Innocent Batsani-Ncube, an associate professor of African politics at Queen Mary University of London and author of the book China and African Parliaments, said what China was doing as a major global power was not fundamentally different from other powers, as all countries sought influence, but Beijing’s strategy was unique in its use of “concrete symbols” or gifted buildings to project influence in the short term.
The long-term plan was to “not just talk to people who are currently in charge of governmental power but to locate themselves within the formal African public institutions so that they can secure long-term access”, Batsani-Ncube said of China’s gifts to regional blocs such as the African Union and Ecowas.
He said China presented those palaces or parliaments in a way that “paralyses the bureaucracy”, severely limiting the recipient’s room for negotiation.
The new Zimbabwean parliament in Mount Hampden, 18km (11 miles) northwest of Harare, was funded by a US$140 million grant from China and built by the Shanghai Construction Group. It was handed over in 2022.
“At the top, when the gift is given, there is very limited space for negotiation,” Batsani-Ncube added.
He said in many cases, China continued to maintain the buildings long after construction, ensuring sustained leverage.
These gifts have not gone without controversy. In 2018, Beijing was accused of bugging the African Union headquarters.
However, Batsani-Ncube dismissed espionage claims as he found “no conclusive evidence” and no realistic basis to believe China would plant listening devices in African parliaments, as their proceedings were typically public.
He said the strategic value lay elsewhere. China’s primary goal in providing infrastructure was the overt “symbolism” of its physical presence and the control it granted, which was a more powerful form of influence, Batsani-Ncube added.

data.title: Beijing protests following reports of Taiwanese official’s secret Israel trip
data.description:
Beijing’s embassy in Israel has protested following reports of a secret trip by Taiwan’s deputy foreign minister to the Middle Eastern country.
Citing several anonymous sources, Reuters reported on Thursday that Francois Wu Chih-chung had travelled to Israel in recent weeks, though it was not disclosed who he met there. Taipei has not officially confirmed that the visit took place.
On Saturday, the Chinese embassy in Israel commented on the visit, stating that Beijing firmly opposed official exchanges between any country it has diplomatic relations with and Taiwan.
“The Chinese embassy in Israel lodged a solemn representation with Israel immediately,” the embassy said in a post on social media.
It said that the one-China principle was a “universal consensus” and the basis for establishing diplomatic relations with all countries, including Israel.
It added that the Taiwan question was one of Beijing’s core interests and a “red line that must not be crossed”.
“We once again urge the Israeli side to earnestly abide by the one-China principle, correct its erroneous actions, cease sending any wrong signals to separatist forces advocating ‘Taiwan independence’, and take concrete actions to safeguard the overall development of China-Israel relations,” the embassy said.
Before becoming Taiwan’s deputy foreign minister last year, Wu served as the island’s de facto ambassador in Paris for six years. His last public appearance abroad was at the Berlin Security Conference on November 19.


Taiwan and Israel do not have formal diplomatic relations but have maintained close unofficial economic, technological and defence ties and have established de facto embassies in Taipei and Tel Aviv.
In October, Taiwanese leader William Lai Ching-te said he wanted the island to build a “T-Dome” missile shield modelled after Israel’s Iron Dome – a statement that was swiftly condemned by Beijing.
Beijing sees the island as part of China that must be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including Israel and the United States, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-governed island by force and is committed to arming Taiwan.
A joint communique on establishing diplomatic relations between Beijing and Israel, signed in 1992, states that Israel recognises the People’s Republic of China as the sole legal government of China, including the island as an “inalienable” territory.
In a social media post on Thursday, Taiwanese Foreign Minister Lin Chia-lung said that Ohad Tal, chair of the Israeli parliament’s Indo-Pacific caucus, had made a recent visit to Taiwan for talks.
Lin said that the two discussed how Taiwan and Israel both faced challenging geopolitical environments and that they were “mutually supportive democratic partners”.
“Recent people-to-people exchanges and parliamentary relations have been very close,” he said, adding that an economic delegation would soon travel to Israel to interact in areas including semiconductors and hi-tech talent development.
Lin said Tal had “long promoted pro-Taiwan efforts in the Israeli Knesset” and planned to continue advocating for Taiwan in international forums.
“Taiwan will continue to work hand in hand with its democratic allies, including Israel, to strive for a safer world and contribute to regional prosperity,” he said.

data.title: How Imogen Poots and Kristen Stewart gave their all to The Chronology of Water
data.description:
Imogen Poots has been thinking about a Sam Shepard quote: “People here have become the people they’re pretending to be.”
Those 10 words, from a poem in his Motel Chronicles collection, are kind of about her character in Hedda, the quietly courageous Thea. But they are also kind of about everything.
After 20 years of acting in films, television and on the stage, Poots is having a clarifying moment. And Shepard’s words somehow get to the heart of it all: the disorienting paradox of attempting to work as an artist in a big industry like Hollywood and preserving your soul in the process.
“I was always clear about what I wanted to do professionally, if only I could get there, in independent cinema and theatre,” Poots says. “But only in the last two years, something’s clicked.”


The 36-year-old English actor has always managed to elegantly navigate her way through the distracting noise of franchises and fame and find the types of interesting filmmakers, stories and projects she had always dreamed of, working with the likes of Peter Bogdanovich, Terrence Malick, Philip Seymour Hoffman and Imelda Staunton along the way.
But this year has been particularly special with three films that she is enormously proud to be part of: sharing the screen with Nina Hoss in Nia DaCosta’s fiery Hedda (streaming on Prime Video); delving into the throes of an affair with Brett Goldstein in the romantic drama All of You (streaming on Apple TV); and giving herself over to what may just be remembered as a defining performance in Kristen Stewart’s directorial debut, The Chronology of Water, which will be released in the US in January.
While all three films had independent spirits, The Chronology of Water was the biggest unknown – a daring and provocative adaptation of Lidia Yuknavitch’s memoir about escaping an abusive home, dashed dreams of competitive swimming, and losing herself in sex, drugs and alcohol before finding her voice through writing.
“This person’s life is so rich with inconsistencies and successes and sabotage. She’s kind of like the weather,” Poots says.
The intensity of it required total trust in Stewart, an artist she had only admired from a few degrees away until they decided to dive into the deep end together.
Work did not stop at wrap, she says. It was always spilling out over texts and calls and videos. And it all just felt right, a creative collaboration like she had never experienced before.
“The two of us only know how to do it this way, which is to completely hurl yourself into it,” Poots says. “The stakes were so high for one another. We didn’t want to let the other one down.”

Someone said something recently to Stewart that she found very illuminating: they never bring the baggage of knowing that Poots has been in other films when watching her in something. It is like they’re always meeting her anew.
“It’s because she’s not like an ‘actress’,” Stewart says. “She’s, like, giving you her life, and it’s full and it’s so generous … you never really know where it’s going to go because she’s not planning it. She’s genuinely trust-falling.”
If there had been any hesitation, Stewart says, there is no way it would have worked. On screen, Poots just feels alive. And that is exactly what Stewart needed: a person who knows how to live life, unselfconsciously.
At the film’s world premiere at Cannes earlier this year, Stewart and Poots sat there shaking – vibrating for one another, wanting the other to soar.
“We really did something,” Poots says. “We did it together with every cell in our body.”

Acting in the film business is hard for all its myriad contradictions, and projects like The Chronology of Water, Hedda and All of You do not come along every day. It demands patience and self-awareness to understand what is right for you and what is not.
“It’s creative and it’s arbitrary in one sense, and in other ways it’s very, very specific and precise,” Poots says. “You’re fighting against this unknowable, very sort of shiftable force, which is trend. And you’re trying to stay authentic in the face of that.”
She thinks of someone like her friend Jesse Eisenberg appearing in a Superman film, and Stewart, who climbed out of Twilight and built a brilliant body of work, and how good you have to be to go back to working with auteurs.
In the beginning of her career, she was happy to take the work where she could, to try new things, to throw paint at the wall, so to speak, and figure out what felt right and what did not. And she looks back on jobs that might not have been the best fit with empathy. It is important to learn what you are not happy doing as well.
“It does not matter if everyone around you is like, ‘But it should be fun,’ like, ‘Go to the party!’” she says.
“You do not have to do that, I don’t think. You do not have to be that uncomfortable. You can find people where you’re on the same page.”

data.title: Gaza amputees desperate to walk again amid medical delays: ‘my future is paralysed’
data.description:
Sitting in her wheelchair, Haneen al-Mabhouh dreams of rebuilding her family, of cradling a new baby. She dreams of walking again. But with her leg gone, her life in Gaza is on hold, she says, as she waits to go abroad for further treatment.
An Israeli air strike in July 2024 smashed her home in central Gaza as she and her family slept. All four of her daughters were killed, including her five-month-old baby.
Her husband was severely burned. Al-Mabhouh’s legs were crushed under the rubble, and doctors had to amputate her right leg above the knee.
“For the past year and a half, I have been unable to move around, to live like others. For the past year and a half, I have been without children,” she said, speaking at her parents’ home.

The two-month-old ceasefire in Gaza has been slow to bring help for thousands of Palestinians who suffered amputations from Israeli bombardment over the past two years.
The World Health Organization (WHO) estimates there are some 5,000 to 6,000 amputees from the war, 25 per cent of them children.
Those who lost limbs are struggling to adapt, faced with a shortage of prosthetic limbs and long delays in medical evacuations out of Gaza.
The WHO said a shipment of essential prosthetic supplies recently made it into Gaza. That appears to be the first significant shipment in the past two years.
Previously, Israel had let in almost no ready-made prosthetic limbs or material to manufacture limbs since the war began, according to Loay Abu Saif, the head of the disability programme at Medical Aid for Palestinians (MAP), and Nevin Al Ghussein, acting director of the Artificial Limbs and Polio Centre in Gaza City.
The Israeli military body in charge of coordinating aid did not respond when asked how many prosthetic supplies had entered during the war or about its policies on such supplies.
Al-Mabhouh was asleep with her baby girl in her arms when the strike hit their home in Nuseirat, she said. For several weeks while recovering in hospital, she had no idea her children had been killed.
She underwent multiple surgeries. Her hand still has difficulty moving. Her remaining leg remains shattered, held together with rods. She needs a bone graft and other treatments that are available only outside Gaza.


She was put on the list for medical evacuation 10 months ago but still has not been given permission to leave Gaza.
Waiting for her chance to go, she lives at her parents’ house. She needs help changing clothes and cannot even hold a pen, and remains crushed by grief over her daughters.
“I never got to hear her say ‘mama’, see her first tooth or watch her take her first steps,” she said of her baby.
She dreams of having a new child but cannot until she gets treatment.
“It’s my right to live, to have another child, to regain what I lost, to walk, just to walk again,” she said. “Now my future is paralysed. They destroyed my dreams.”
No increase in medical evacuations
The ceasefire has hardly brought any increase in medical evacuations for the 16,500 Palestinians the UN says are waiting to get vital treatment abroad – not just amputees, but patients suffering many kinds of chronic conditions or wounds.
As of December 1, 235 patients have been evacuated since the ceasefire began in October, just under five a day. In the months before that, the average was about three a day.
Israel last week said it was ready to allow patients and other Palestinians to leave Gaza via the Israeli-held Rafah crossing between Gaza and Egypt.

But it is unsure that will happen because Egypt, which controls the crossing’s other side, demands Rafah also be opened for Palestinians to enter Gaza as called for under the ceasefire deal.
Dr Richard Peeperkorn, the WHO’s representative in the occupied Palestinian territory, said that the backlog was caused by the lack of countries to host the evacuated patients.
He said new medevac routes needed to be opened, especially to the Israeli-occupied West Bank and East Jerusalem, where hospitals are ready to receive patients.
Yassin Marouf lies in a tent in central Gaza, his left foot amputated, his right leg barely held together with rods.
The 23-year-old and his brother were hit by Israeli shelling in May as they returned from visiting their home in northern Gaza that their family had been forced to flee. His brother was killed. Marouf lay bleeding on the ground, as a stray dog attacked his mangled left leg.
Doctors say his right leg will also need to be amputated, unless he can travel abroad for operations that might save it. Marouf said he could not afford painkillers and could not go to the hospital regularly to have his bandages changed as they were supposed to.
“If I want to go to the bathroom, I need two or three people to carry me,” he said.

Mohamed al-Naggar had been pursuing an IT degree at the University of Palestine before the war.
Seven months ago, shrapnel pierced his left leg during strikes on the house where his family was sheltering. Doctors amputated his leg above the knee. His right leg was also badly injured and shrapnel remains in parts of his body.
Despite four surgeries and physiotherapy, the 21-year-old cannot move around.
“I’d like to travel abroad and put on a prosthetic and graduate from college and be normal like young people outside Gaza,” he said.
Shortage of limbs
Some 42,000 Palestinians have suffered life-changing injuries in the war, including amputations, brain trauma, spinal cord injuries and major burns, the WHO said in an October report.
The situation has “improved slightly” for those with assistance needs but “there is still a huge overall shortage of assistive products,” such as wheelchairs, walking frames and crutches.
Gaza has only eight prosthetists able to manufacture and fit artificial limbs, according to the WHO.
The Artificial Limbs and Polio Center in Gaza City, one of two prosthetics centres still operating in the territory, received a shipment of material to manufacture limbs just before the war began in 2023, said its director, Al Ghussein. Another small shipment entered in December 2024, but nothing since.

The centre has been able to provide artificial limbs for 250 cases over the course of the war, but supplies are running out, Al Ghussein said.
No pre-made prosthetic legs or arms have entered, according to Abu Saif of MAP. Though Israel does not ban them, its procedures cause delays and “in the end they ignore it”.
Ibrahim Khalif wants a prosthetic right leg so he can get a job doing manual labour or cleaning houses to support his pregnant wife and children.
In January, he lost his leg when an Israeli air strike hit Gaza City while he was out getting food.
“I used to be the provider for my kids, but now I’m sitting here,” Khalif said. “I think of how I was and what I’ve become.”
